summary: At Columbia University, I studied and practiced teaching, which was a formative period for me. During that time, I also made marionettes as a creative outlet, helping shape my approach to both art and education. The experience there laid the foundation for my future work in fiber arts and teaching.

I served in the United States Army in Alaska, which was an important chapter in my life. This experience came after I had been teaching in Puyallup, Washington, and it influenced my perspective and discipline. My time in the Army was part of the journey that led me deeper into teaching and fiber arts.

During my service in the United States Army, I was stationed in Alaska. This period was significant as it came between my teaching roles and my further studies in design and weaving. Alaska was a unique setting that contributed to my life experience before I moved on to Cranbrook Academy of Art.

At Cranbrook Academy of Art, I deepened my understanding of design and weaving, which was crucial for my career. This came after my Army service and teaching in Puyallup, marking a period of growth in my artistic skills. Cranbrook helped me refine my craft and prepared me for future teaching roles.

Teaching weaving at the University of Washington was a significant step in my career. It followed my time at Cranbrook and allowed me to engage with students in a meaningful way. This role helped me develop my teaching philosophy and connect with the fiber arts community in the Pacific Northwest.

I was invited to teach at the University of California, Berkeley, which was a wonderful opportunity. There, I worked within the College of Letters and Science and later in the Department of Architecture, benefiting from the diverse teaching styles of the faculty. Berkeley was a vibrant environment that enriched my teaching and artistic practice.

At Berkeley, I initially worked in the College of Letters and Science, where I began my teaching there. This position allowed me to explore interdisciplinary approaches and engage with a broad range of students. It was an important part of my academic career before moving to the Department of Architecture.

entity: Department of Architecture, University of California, Berkeley
summary: Later in my tenure at Berkeley, I taught in the Department of Architecture, which offered a unique perspective on design and creativity. This role complemented my fiber arts background and allowed me to collaborate with faculty who had diverse teaching styles. It was a stimulating environment that broadened my educational impact.

Dorothy Liebes was a major influence on me and the fiber arts movement in the Bay Area. Her studio was known for generating innovative fiber concepts and designs for industry, which inspired my own work. I admired her creativity and how she bridged art and industrial design during my time teaching in California.

Trude Guermonprez was a remarkable teacher at the California College of Arts and Crafts and had a significant impact on the local fiber arts scene. Her teaching and work influenced me as I developed my own approach to fiber arts. Being part of the Bay Area community, her presence helped shape the movement we were all part of.
cues: California College of Arts and Crafts

summary: The California College of the Arts was home to influential fiber artists like Trude Guermonprez, whose teaching shaped the local arts scene. Though I taught elsewhere, the college's culture and faculty were important to the Bay Area fiber arts movement. It represented a hub of creativity and innovation during my time in California.

I taught in Puyallup, Washington, before my Army service, which was an early teaching experience for me. This role helped me develop my skills and commitment to education in fiber arts. Puyallup was an important stepping stone that led to my later studies and teaching positions.
